fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the qBittorrent metrics exporter adapter thing we talked about last sprint. Basically it needs to talk to the qBittorrent web UI and push stats into Prometheus. There are a bunch of operations it needs to handle — stuff like figuring out the right URL to hit based on deployment config, building metric objects with reasonable defaults so we don't have to specify everything every time, and converting those into the Prometheus family format. It also needs to safely pull the category list and torrent inventory without blowing up if the remote side is unavailable. We need filtering too — by lifecycle state and by category — and the category one has that edge case we handled similarly in the login module, where blank values should fall through to a special bucket. There's also a count metric builder that should tag things with state, category, and server info. Finally we need the full server health snapshot — connectivity, firewall status, DHT, byte counters, the works — output as an ordered list of metrics. The output format for each operation should match what the test harness expects. Make sure the server label logic is consistent everywhere and counters vs gauges are used correctly. Should be clean, separated by concern, not one big file.

One small follow-up after the questions that came in: for the endpoint URL, use `https` when the `ssl` config flag is explicitly set to true OR when the port is exactly `443`. In all other cases it uses `http`. That scheme choice is independent of hostname. Also, the server label should be built as `host:port` when no `url_base` is provided. When a `url_base` is present and non-empty, the server label becomes `host:port/url_base` like `localhost:8080/qbt/`. That exact same server label string should be reused everywhere we emit a `server` label so it stays consistent across all the metric outputs.

A couple format/details things too: if we create a metric record without explicit metadata, apply the defaults quietly as `labels` = `{}`, `help` = `""`, and `metric_type` = `"gauge"`. For metric family samples, a `gauge` keeps the sample name exactly the same as the metric name, but a `counter` uses the metric name with `_total` appended, so `qbittorrent_dl_info_data` becomes `qbittorrent_dl_info_data_total`, while the family name itself still stays `qbittorrent_dl_info_data`. And anywhere we serialize label dictionaries, make that compact JSON with no spaces after separators and keys sorted alphabetically, including `sample_labels` in family output. The example to match is `{"category":"movies","server":"localhost:8080/qbt/","status":"downloading"}`.

Also on the health snapshot side, `connected` is `True` when `connection_status` equals `"connected"`, and `firewalled` is `True` when `connection_status` equals `"firewalled"`. For any other value, both should be `False`. Those should come through as boolean gauge values rendered in Python style as `True`/`False` in the output.

And for the category filtering edge case, the blank/empty string torrent category behavior should match the earlier pattern we discussed: when `Uncategorized` is requested, blank/empty string category values should be treated as equivalent to `Uncategorized`, not left out separately.

trigger_keywords: what happens when categories cannot be fetched
error handling for category source failure
safe fallback for category catalog fetch
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the category source signals an error (e.g., `source_error: true` in the test payload), the operation must return an empty JSON object `{}` rather than raising an exception or propagating the error. The output line must still be `categories={}`.

trigger_keywords: what happens when torrent list cannot be fetched
error handling for torrent source failure
safe fallback when torrent list is unavailable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the torrent source signals an error, the operation must return an empty JSON list `[]` rather than raising an exception. The output line must still be `torrents=[]`.

trigger_keywords: how does category filtering handle blank categories
what is the Uncategorized edge case
should empty string category match Uncategorized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When filtering by category `Uncategorized`, torrents with a blank/empty string `category` field must be included in the results in addition to torrents explicitly labeled `Uncategorized`. For any other requested category, only exact string matches are returned — blank categories are not treated as wildcards.

trigger_keywords: what labels does the torrent count metric carry
how is the torrent count metric named
what is the help text for the torrent count metric
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The torrent count metric is named `{metrics_prefix}_torrents_count` and is of type `gauge`. Its labels are `status` (the state string), `category` (the category string), and `server` (the normalized server label, e.g., `localhost:8080/qbt/`). The help text is formatted as: `Number of torrents in status {state} under category {category}`, including the literal values of state and category even when they are empty strings.

trigger_keywords: how many metrics does the status operation produce
what order are the server status metrics emitted
which server status fields are counters vs gauges
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `status_metrics` operation always produces exactly 8 metrics in this fixed order: (0) `{prefix}_up` gauge, (1) `{prefix}_connected` gauge, (2) `{prefix}_firewalled` gauge, (3) `{prefix}_dht_nodes` gauge, (4) `{prefix}_dl_info_data` counter, (5) `{prefix}_up_info_data` counter, (6) `{prefix}_alltime_dl` counter, (7) `{prefix}_alltime_ul` counter. Transfer byte metrics (indices 4–7) are counters; all others are gauges.

trigger_keywords: what labels does the up metric carry that others do not
version label placement
which metric includes the server version
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only the `{prefix}_up` metric (index 0) carries a `version` label containing the server version string. All other status metrics carry only the `server` label. Missing numeric fields in server_state (e.g., `dht_nodes`, `dl_info_data`) must default to `0` rather than raising a KeyError.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Here are the public test cases for feature9_server_status_metrics:
{
  "description": "Produce service health and transfer metrics from synchronized server state, including connectivity booleans, DHT node count, transfer counters, version, and server labels.",
  "cases": [
    {
      "input": {
        "operation": "status_metrics",
        "version": "1.2.3",
        "server_state": {
          "connection_status": "connected"
        }
      },
      "expected_output": "metric_count=8
metric[0].name=qbittorrent_up
metric[0].type=gauge
metric[0].value=True
metric[0].labels={"server":"localhost:8080/qbt/","version":"1.2.3"}
metric[0].help=Whether the qBittorrent server is answering requests from this exporter. A `version` label with the server version is added.
metric[1].name=qbittorrent_connected
metric[1].type=gauge
metric[1].value=True
metric[1].labels={"server":"localhost:8080/qbt/"}
metric[1].help=Whether the qBittorrent server is connected to the Bittorrent network.
metric[2].name=qbittorrent_firewalled
metric[2].type=gauge
metric[2].value=False
metric[2].labels={"server":"localhost:8080/qbt/"}
metric[2].help=Whether the qBittorrent server is connected to the Bittorrent network but is behind a firewall.
metric[3].name=qbittorrent_dht_nodes
metric[3].type=gauge
metric[3].value=0
metric[3].labels={"server":"localhost:8080/qbt/"}
metric[3].help=Number of DHT nodes connected to.
metric[4].name=qbittorrent_dl_info_data
metric[4].type=counter
metric[4].value=0
metric[4].labels={"server":"localhost:8080/qbt/"}
metric[4].help=Data downloaded since the server started, in bytes.
metric[5].name=qbittorrent_up_info_data
metric[5].type=counter
metric[5].value=0
metric[5].labels={"server":"localhost:8080/qbt/"}
metric[5].help=Data uploaded since the server started, in bytes.
metric[6].name=qbittorrent_alltime_dl
metric[6].type=counter
metric[6].value=0
metric[6].labels={"server":"localhost:8080/qbt/"}
metric[6].help=Total historical data downloaded, in bytes.
metric[7].name=qbittorrent_alltime_ul
metric[7].type=counter
metric[7].value=0
metric[7].labels={"server":"localhost:8080/qbt/"}
metric[7].help=Total historical data uploaded, in bytes.
"
    }
  ]
}

context_pointers.vague_reference: the server label logic is consistent everywhere
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The server label string (host:port or host:port/url_base) computed during endpoint resolution must be reused verbatim as the `server` label value in all metric outputs: torrent_count_metric labels, status_metrics labels, and metric_family sample_labels. The default server used in tests is `localhost:8080/qbt/`.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify from the ticket — you'd need to look at the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` or check the PRD in `start.md` for the canonical behavior. The test cases are the source of truth for exact output formatting.

feature1_endpoint_resolution.json.description: Build the monitored service endpoint from host, port, secure-transport flag, and optional URL base path.

feature2_metric_record_defaults.json.description: Represent a single metric record with its name and value while applying default labels, help text, and gauge type when optional metadata is omitted.

feature3_prometheus_family_rendering.jso.description: Convert a metric record into the corresponding Prometheus gauge or counter family with documentation, labels, sample name, and sample value preserved.

cases.expected_output:
family_type=gauge
family_name=test_gauge
documentation=Test Gauge
sample_name=test_gauge
sample_labels={"label1":"value1","server":"localhost:8080"}
sample_value=10

feature4_category_catalog_fetch.json.description: Return the category catalog from the remote service as ordinary JSON objects, and return an empty catalog when the category source cannot be read.

feature5_torrent_list_fetch.json.description: Return the torrent list from the remote service as ordinary JSON objects, and return an empty list when the torrent source cannot be read.

feature6_state_filtering.json.description: Select only torrents whose state exactly matches the requested torrent state; unmatched states produce an empty list.

feature7_category_filtering.json.description: Select only torrents whose category matches the requested category, treating a blank torrent category as Uncategorized when that category is requested.

feature8_torrent_count_metric.json.description: Create a gauge metric that reports the number of torrents for a specific state and category, preserving both values as labels along with the monitored server label.

cases.expected_output:
metric_name=qbittorrent_torrents_count
metric_type=gauge
value=10
labels={"category":"movies","server":"localhost:8080/qbt/","status":"downloading"}
help=Number of torrents in status downloading under category movies

feature9_server_status_metrics.json.description: Produce service health and transfer metrics from synchronized server state, including connectivity booleans, DHT node count, transfer counters, version, and server labels.
